source_intake_record.raw_question_text: A flyball governor consists of two masses $m$ connected to arms of length $l$ and a mass $M$ as shown in Fig. 2.68. The assembly is constrained to rotate around a shaft on which the mass $M$ can slide up and down without friction. Neglect the mass of the arms, air friction, and assume that the diameter of the mass $M$ is small. Suppose first that the shaft is constrained to rotate at an angular velocity $\omega_{0}$. Calculate the equilibrium height of the mass $M$.
source_intake_record.raw_answer_text: There are two equilibrium positions: one with height $2l$ (when $\theta_{0}=0$) and the nontrivial one with $2l\cos\theta_{0}=\frac{2(m+M)g}{m\omega_{0}^{2}}$ (with $\cos\theta_{0}=\frac{(m+M)g}{ml\omega_{0}^{2}}$).
extraction_notes: images字段中提供的是PIL图像对象字符串表示，不是实际文件路径或URL

The provided answer includes two equilibrium cases (trivial and nontrivial), which suggests case analysis rather than a single direct lookup
The answer expresses both an intermediate angular relation and the final height relation, indicating at least two reasoning steps

`answer_present` is true and the answer text specifies the equilibrium conditions clearly
`registry_passed` is true, so the asset registration pipeline did not fail
Although the answer is free text, it contains concrete mathematical expressions rather than open-ended explanation

candidate_problem_record.subject: 物理
candidate_problem_record.raw_question_text: A flyball governor consists of two masses $m$ connected to arms of length $l$ and a mass $M$ as shown in Fig. 2.68. The assembly is constrained to rotate around a shaft on which the mass $M$ can slide up and down without friction. Neglect the mass of the arms, air friction, and assume that the diameter of the mass $M$ is small. Suppose first that the shaft is constrained to rotate at an angular velocity $\omega_{0}$. Calculate the equilibrium height of the mass $M$.
candidate_problem_record.raw_answer_text: There are two equilibrium positions: one with height $2l$ (when $\theta_{0}=0$) and the nontrivial one with $2l\cos\theta_{0}=\frac{2(m+M)g}{m\omega_{0}^{2}}$ (with $\cos\theta_{0}=\frac{(m+M)g}{ml\omega_{0}^{2}}$).
candidate_problem_record.multi_solution_policy_rationale: 该数据集保留多解潜力评估，但不默认强推多解 agent。

extraction_notes: images字段中提供的是PIL图像对象字符串表示，不是实际文件路径或URL
metadata.reasoning_chain: At equilibrium, $\ddot{\theta}=0$, $\dot{\theta}=0$, $\theta=\theta_{0}$ and the above becomes $$m l \omega_{0}^{2}\sin2\theta_{0}=2(m+M)g\sin\theta_{0}.$$ Solving for $\theta_{0}$ we obtain two equilibrium positions: (i) $\theta_{0}=0$, (ii) $\cos\theta_{0}=\frac{(m+M)g}{ml\omega_{0}^{2}}$. The distances of the mass $M$ at the two equilibrium positions from the top of the shaft are respectively: (i) $2l\cos\theta_{0}=2l$, (ii) $2l\cos\theta_{0}=\frac{2(m+M)g}{m\omega_{0}^{2}}$.
